notes:
tám means eight.

It’s a number, so you put it before a classifier + noun when counting things.  

Vietnamese numbers don’t change form, so tám stays the same in all positions. It has a rising tone (sắc), so your voice goes up slightly.

Nearby numbers to anchor it: bảy (7), tám (8), chín (9).
exampleSentences: Anh có thể mua cho em tám quả táo ở siêu thị không?
Gia đình chị có tám người nên bàn ăn rất lớn.
Em dậy lúc tám giờ sáng để chuẩn bị đi học.
exampleSentencesTranslated: Can you buy eight apples for me at the supermarket?
There are eight people in my family so the dining table is very big.
I wake up at eight in the morning to get ready for school.
notes_md:
**tám** means **eight**.

It’s a number, so you put it before a classifier + noun when counting things.  

Vietnamese numbers don’t change form, so *tám* stays the same in all positions. It has a rising tone (sắc), so your voice goes up slightly.

Nearby numbers to anchor it: **bảy** (7), **tám** (8), **chín** (9).
